overview.introduction: This capstone applies everything you have learned across all four Linux modules into a single realistic DevOps scenario: configuring a Linux server from scratch — directory structure, user permissions, shell scripts, and network validation.
overview.why_it_matters: Real interviews and real jobs don't test commands in isolation. They test whether you can combine ls, chmod, bash, ps, ss, and curl to solve an actual problem under pressure.
overview.industry_usage: The tasks in this capstone mirror day-1 activities for a junior DevOps engineer: provisioning a server, setting up deployment automation, configuring permissions, and validating the environment.
overview.what_you_will_build: A complete server setup simulation: project directory structure, protected config files with correct permissions, a deployment validation script, and a network connectivity health check.
overview.prerequisites: All four previous modules: Linux CLI Basics, File Permissions, Bash Scripting, and Networking & Processes.
learning_outcomes: Combine CLI navigation, file operations, and permissions in a realistic workflow.
Write a multi-step deployment validation script from scratch.
Apply correct permissions to configs, scripts, and directories.
Perform process and port verification as part of a server health check.
Document and present your work as a completed DevOps project.

key_concepts.title: Combining Commands: The Unix Philosophy
key_concepts.definition: Linux commands are designed to be combined using pipes (|) and redirection (>, >>) to perform complex tasks. This is the Unix Philosophy: small tools that do one thing well, chained together.
why_important: Complex tasks are solved by combining simple commands — not writing new programs.
Pipes let you process data through multiple steps in a single command line.
Redirection saves command output to files for logging and reporting.
Command substitution $() captures output to use as input for another step.
key_concepts.explanation:
Pipes pass the output of one command to the input of the next:

```bash
ps aux | grep nginx | awk '{print $2}'
```
Step 1: ps aux — list all processes
Step 2: grep nginx — filter for nginx lines
Step 3: awk '{print $2}' — extract just the PID column

Chaining commands conditionally:

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `\|` | Pipe stdout to next cmd | `ls \| grep .log` |
| `>` | Redirect stdout to file | `ls > files.txt` |
| `>>` | Append stdout to file | `ls >> files.txt` |
| `&&` | Run next only if first succeeds | `mkdir dir && cd dir` |
| `\|\|` | Run next only if first fails | `mkdir dir \|\| echo 'exists'` |
| `;` | Run both regardless of exit code | `cmd1; cmd2` |

commands.name: Pipe (|)
commands.purpose: Connects the output of one command to the input of the next.
commands.parameters: Each command in the chain processes the output of the previous one.
commands.example: ps aux | grep -v grep | grep nginx | wc -l
commands.output_explanation: This pipeline counts how many nginx processes are running. ps aux → filter out the grep process itself → filter for nginx → count lines.
commands.when_to_use: Use pipes whenever you need to filter, transform, or count command output.
commands.real_world_use: count running docker containers: docker ps | wc -l

key_concepts.pro_tip: Use `tee` to both display output AND save it to a file simultaneously: `command | tee output.log`
key_concepts.best_practices: Build complex pipelines step by step — test each stage before adding the next pipe.
key_concepts.common_mistakes: Using ; instead of && for dependent steps — if mkdir fails with ;, cd runs anyway and succeeds incorrectly.
key_concepts.real_world_example: A DevOps engineer runs a single pipeline: `ps aux | grep java | grep -v grep | awk '{print $2}' | xargs kill` to kill all java processes in one command.
key_concepts.remember: Each command in a pipeline runs in its own subshell. Variable assignments in a pipeline don't persist after the pipe.
key_concepts.summary: The pipe | chains commands. && chains dependent steps. || provides fallback. These are the building blocks of automation.

key_concepts.title: Capstone Project Architecture
key_concepts.definition: The capstone simulates a real server provisioning task — creating the structure, securing it, scripting it, and verifying it.
why_important: Reinforces every concept from all four modules in one connected workflow.
Demonstrates competency to employers — this is a real-world DevOps task.
Builds the muscle memory of the correct order: structure → permissions → script → verify.
Creates a reference project you can explain in interviews.
key_concepts.explanation:
Project structure you will build:

```
~/devops-project/
├── app/
│   ├── src/             # Application source
│   └── config/
│       └── app.env      # Protected (600)
├── scripts/
│   ├── deploy.sh        # Executable (755)
│   ├── healthcheck.sh   # Executable (755)
│   └── setup.sh         # Executable (755)
├── logs/
│   └── deploy.log       # Log output
└── backups/             # Config backups
```

**Workflow:**
1. Create the directory structure (mkdir -p)
2. Write config files and set 600 permissions
3. Write and test bash scripts
4. Set correct script permissions (755)
5. Run health checks and save output to logs
6. Verify everything with ls -la

commands.name: mkdir -p (nested directories)
commands.purpose: Creates an entire nested directory tree in a single command.
commands.parameters: -p: create parent directories as needed, no error if already exists.
commands.output_explanation: The entire project directory tree is created. Use ls -la ~/devops-project/ to verify.
commands.when_to_use: Always use -p when creating nested structures. Without it, mkdir fails if the parent doesn't exist.
commands.real_world_use: Provisioning scripts use mkdir -p to create the complete directory layout for an application in one step.

key_concepts.pro_tip: Use `tree` command (if available) to visualize the entire directory structure: `tree ~/devops-project/`
key_concepts.best_practices: Always create directory structures before writing files into them. Start with the full tree before any content.
key_concepts.common_mistakes: Creating directories one at a time — mkdir -p handles the entire tree in one command.
key_concepts.real_world_example: A Terraform null_resource provisioner runs a script that creates the entire application directory structure with mkdir -p before copying binaries.
key_concepts.remember: mkdir -p never fails if the directory already exists. This makes it safe to re-run provisioning scripts.
key_concepts.summary: Plan your directory structure first, then create it all with mkdir -p. Build depth before breadth.

interactive_examples.objective: Build the complete project directory structure.
interactive_examples.scenario: You are provisioning a new server for a web application. The first step is always creating the directory layout.
interactive_examples.explanation: Brace expansion {a,b,c} creates multiple paths at once. mkdir -p handles nested paths. ls -la verifies the structure.
interactive_examples.expected_output:
total 28
drwxr-xr-x 6 student student 4096 Jan 12 .
drwxr-xr-x 5 student student 4096 Jan 12 ..
drwxr-xr-x 4 student student 4096 Jan 12 app
drwxr-xr-x 2 student student 4096 Jan 12 backups
drwxr-xr-x 2 student student 4096 Jan 12 logs
drwxr-xr-x 2 student student 4096 Jan 12 scripts
interactive_examples.output_explanation: All four top-level directories are created. The app directory contains src and config subdirectories.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Server provisioning playbooks (Ansible) and Terraform provisioners use this exact pattern to set up application directories.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Creating directories without -p — if a parent doesn't exist, mkdir fails with 'No such file or directory'.
interactive_examples.tips: Brace expansion {a,b} is a powerful shortcut: mkdir {dir1,dir2,dir3} creates three directories in one command.

interactive_examples.objective: Create and protect a configuration file.
interactive_examples.scenario: Write the application's environment variables into a config file and immediately lock it down so only the owner can read it.
interactive_examples.explanation: echo writes config values. chmod 600 immediately restricts access. ls -l verifies -rw------- permissions.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: -rw------- confirms only the owner can read or write this file. No one else has any access.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Never leave .env files at 644. Always chmod 600 immediately after creation — before any other user could read it.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Writing the config first, then forgetting to chmod 600 — leaving it readable by other users.
interactive_examples.tips: Chain the commands: echo '...' > .env && chmod 600 .env — use && so chmod only runs if echo succeeds.

interactive_examples.scenario: Create a script that checks if all required files and directories exist before a deployment proceeds.
interactive_examples.command:
cat > ~/devops-project/scripts/validate.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
set -e
echo 'Running pre-deployment validation...'
[ -f ~/devops-project/app/config/app.env ] && echo '[OK] Config file found' || echo '[FAIL] Config file missing'
[ -d ~/devops-project/logs ] && echo '[OK] Logs directory found' || echo '[FAIL] Logs directory missing'
[ -d ~/devops-project/backups ] && echo '[OK] Backups directory found' || echo '[FAIL] Backups directory missing'
echo 'Validation complete.'
EOF
chmod 755 ~/devops-project/scripts/validate.sh && ~/devops-project/scripts/validate.sh
interactive_examples.explanation: Heredoc (<<'EOF') writes multi-line content to a file. chmod 755 makes it executable. Running it immediately tests all three checks.
interactive_examples.expected_output:
Running pre-deployment validation...
[OK] Config file found
[OK] Logs directory found
[OK] Backups directory found
Validation complete.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: All three checks passed. The script correctly detected existing directories and the config file.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Every production deployment pipeline includes a pre-flight validation script that runs before any changes are applied.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Not setting chmod 755 on the script before running it — results in Permission denied.
interactive_examples.tips: Use [OK]/[FAIL] prefixes in script output — makes it easy to grep for failures in CI logs.

interactive_examples.objective: Log a deployment event with timestamp.
interactive_examples.scenario: Your deployment script should write a timestamped entry to a log file whenever it runs.
interactive_examples.command: TIMESTAMP=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S') && USER=$(whoami) && echo "[$TIMESTAMP] Deployment triggered by $USER" >> ~/devops-project/logs/deploy.log && cat ~/devops-project/logs/deploy.log
interactive_examples.explanation: Timestamp and user are captured with command substitution. >> appends to the log file. cat verifies the entry was written.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: The log entry shows exactly when and who triggered the deployment.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Production deployment logs are audit trails — they record every deployment event for compliance and debugging.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Using > instead of >> — this overwrites the log file with each deployment, destroying the audit trail.
interactive_examples.tips: Append the exit code too: echo "[$TIMESTAMP] Deployment by $USER - EXIT: $?" >> deploy.log

interactive_examples.objective: Run a complete server health check.
interactive_examples.scenario: After setup is complete, verify the server is in a healthy state before going live.
interactive_examples.command: echo '=== SERVER HEALTH CHECK ===' && echo "User: $(whoami)" && echo "Hostname: $(hostname)" && echo "Uptime: $(uptime -p)" && echo "Project dir exists: $(ls -d ~/devops-project 2>/dev/null && echo YES || echo NO)" && echo "Open ports:" && ss -lntp | grep LISTEN
interactive_examples.explanation: Each echo combines a label with command substitution to report system state. The final ss -lntp shows listening ports. 2>/dev/null suppresses errors if the directory doesn't exist.
interactive_examples.expected_output:
=== SERVER HEALTH CHECK ===
User: student
Hostname: devlab
Uptime: up 2 days, 3 hours
Project dir exists: YES
Open ports:
LISTEN  0  128  0.0.0.0:22  users:(("sshd",pid=890,fd=3))
interactive_examples.output_explanation: The health check report shows system identity, uptime, project directory status, and open network ports.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: SRE teams run health check scripts after every server provisioning to validate the environment before handing it off.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Running health checks only once — health checks should be automated and run on a schedule (cron jobs).
interactive_examples.tips: Save the health report to a file: ./healthcheck.sh | tee /var/log/healthcheck.log

lessons.title: 1. Create Project Structure
lessons.definition: A well-organized directory structure is the foundation of any DevOps project.
lessons.explanation: Use mkdir -p to create nested directories: app, scripts, logs, and backups.
lessons.instruction: Create a directory ~/devops-project with subdirectories: app, scripts, logs, backups.

lessons.title: 2. Create Protected Config
lessons.definition: Application configuration files containing credentials must be permission 600.
lessons.explanation: Create app.env with environment variables and immediately set 600 permissions.
lessons.instruction: Create ~/devops-project/app/app.env with content 'ENV=production' and set permissions to 600.

lessons.title: 3. Write and Execute Deploy Script
lessons.definition: Deployment scripts automate multi-step processes as a single executable file.
lessons.explanation: Create a deploy.sh script that echoes 'Deployment starting...' and set it to chmod 755.
lessons.instruction: Create ~/devops-project/scripts/deploy.sh with shebang and an echo statement. Make it executable.

lessons.title: 4. Log a Deployment Event
lessons.definition: Deployment logs record when and by whom changes were made to a system.
lessons.explanation: Capture the timestamp with $() and write to deploy.log using >>.
lessons.instruction: Append a timestamped deployment entry to ~/devops-project/logs/deploy.log.

lessons.title: 5. Backup Config File
lessons.definition: Backing up config files before changes ensures you can recover if something breaks.
lessons.explanation: Copy app.env to backups/ with a timestamped filename.

lessons.title: 6. Verify Project Permissions
lessons.definition: Permission auditing ensures that all files have the correct access levels before going live.
lessons.explanation: Run ls -la recursively to verify permissions across your entire project structure.
lessons.instruction: List all files in ~/devops-project/ with permissions using ls -la.

lessons.title: 7. Run Process Health Check
lessons.definition: Verifying running processes confirms services started correctly after deployment.
lessons.explanation: Use ps aux and ss -lntp to check what is running and what ports are open.
lessons.instruction: Check all listening ports using ss -lntp.

lessons.title: 8. Test Network Connectivity
lessons.definition: Connectivity tests verify that the server can reach required external services.
lessons.explanation: Use ping to test the network stack and curl to test HTTP endpoints.
lessons.instruction: Ping localhost 4 times to verify network stack is functional.

lessons.title: 9. Write a Complete Health Check Script
lessons.definition: A health check script combines multiple verification steps into a single executable report.
lessons.explanation: Write healthcheck.sh that verifies: user identity, project directory exists, app.env is 600, and lists listening ports.
lessons.instruction: Create and run ~/devops-project/scripts/healthcheck.sh that reports environment status.

lessons.title: 10. Final Project Audit
lessons.definition: A final audit verifies the complete project is correctly structured, secured, and documented.
lessons.explanation: Run ls -laR to recursively list all files with permissions across the entire project.
lessons.instruction: Recursively list all files and permissions in ~/devops-project/.

exercises.title: Project Directory Audit
exercises.problem: List all files in the devops-project recursively and verify: app.env is 600, deploy.sh and healthcheck.sh are 755, and all directories are 755.
exercises.hint: Use ls -laR ~/devops-project/. Check permissions visually: -rw------- is 600, -rwxr-xr-x is 755.
exercises.expected_result: Correct permissions on all files as specified.
exercises.objective: Practice permission auditing — a key SRE skill.

exercises.title: Deployment Log Analysis
exercises.problem: Your deploy.log contains multiple entries. Count how many deployments occurred using wc -l. Then show only the last 3 entries using tail.
exercises.hint: wc -l ~/devops-project/logs/deploy.log counts lines. tail -3 ~/devops-project/logs/deploy.log shows last 3.
exercises.expected_result: Line count and last 3 log entries displayed.
exercises.objective: Practice log analysis — critical for post-deployment reviews.

exercises.title: Add Argument Support to Deploy Script
exercises.problem: Modify deploy.sh to accept one argument (environment name). If no argument given, print usage and exit 1. If given, print 'Deploying to ENV'.
exercises.hint: Add `if [ $# -eq 0 ]; then echo 'Usage: deploy.sh <env>'; exit 1; fi` then `echo "Deploying to $1"`
exercises.expected_result: ./deploy.sh staging prints 'Deploying to staging'. Without args prints usage.
exercises.objective: Add argument validation to scripts — an essential production scripting pattern.

exercises.title: Automated Config Backup
exercises.problem: Write a script backup.sh that copies app.env to backups/ with a timestamp in the filename (e.g., app.env.20260112_1030). Verify the backup exists.
exercises.expected_result: A timestamped backup file appears in the backups/ directory.
exercises.objective: Implement a timestamped backup pattern used in real deployment workflows.

exercises.title: Process Count Reporter
exercises.problem: Write a script that reports: total running processes, your own processes count, and current system load average.
exercises.hint: Total: ps aux | wc -l. Your processes: ps aux | grep $(whoami) | wc -l. Load: cat /proc/loadavg or uptime.
exercises.expected_result: Three-line report with process counts and load average.
exercises.objective: Combine process inspection commands into a meaningful report.

exercises.title: Port Availability Checker
exercises.problem: Write a script check_port.sh that accepts a port number as an argument. It should check if that port is in LISTEN state and print OPEN or CLOSED.
exercises.hint: Use `ss -lntp | grep :$1` in the if condition. If it finds a match, print OPEN; else CLOSED.
exercises.objective: Build a reusable port checker utility script.

exercises.title: Complete Deployment Simulation
exercises.problem: Write a full deploy.sh that: (1) validates .env exists, (2) backs up current config, (3) logs the deployment, (4) checks port 22 is listening, (5) prints 'Deployment successful'.
exercises.hint: Combine file existence checks ([ -f ]), cp for backup, echo >> for logging, ss -lntp | grep for port check, all with && or if/else.
exercises.expected_result: Script runs all 5 steps sequentially and prints 'Deployment successful' at the end.
exercises.objective: Build a production-quality multi-step deployment script from scratch.

exercises.title: Rollback Script
exercises.problem: Write rollback.sh that: checks if a backup exists in backups/, copies it back over app.env, logs the rollback event, and confirms the config was restored.
exercises.hint: Find the latest backup with ls backups/app.env.* | sort | tail -1. Restore with cp. Log with echo >> deploy.log.
exercises.expected_result: Rollback script restores the most recent backup and logs the event.
exercises.objective: Implement a rollback mechanism — an essential safety feature in all deployment pipelines.

exercises.title: Cron Job Setup
exercises.problem: Configure your healthcheck.sh to run every 5 minutes using cron. Check the crontab entry, then verify the script produces valid output.
exercises.expected_result: crontab -l shows the entry. health.log grows every 5 minutes.
exercises.objective: Understand how to schedule automation with cron — used for health monitoring and backup jobs.

exercises.title: Full Server Provisioning Script
exercises.problem: Write a single script provision.sh that: creates the complete project directory structure, writes app.env, sets all correct permissions (600 for .env, 755 for scripts), and runs the health check. The script should be completely idempotent (safe to run multiple times).
exercises.hint: Use mkdir -p (safe to re-run), conditional creation for files (if [ ! -f .env ]), chmod on all files, and call ./healthcheck.sh at the end.
exercises.expected_result: Running provision.sh twice produces the same result with no errors.
exercises.objective: Build an idempotent server provisioning script — the foundation of Infrastructure as Code.

quiz.question: What does the `&&` operator do in `mkdir dir && cd dir`?
options: Runs cd dir only if mkdir dir succeeded
Runs mkdir dir only if cd dir fails
Combines the two commands into one
quiz.answer: Runs cd dir only if mkdir dir succeeded
quiz.explanation: && is 'AND' — the second command runs only if the first exits with code 0 (success). If mkdir fails, cd is skipped.

incorrect_explanations.Runs both commands simultaneously: & (single ampersand) runs in background. && is sequential conditional.
incorrect_explanations.Combines the two commands into one: && doesn't combine — it chains sequentially with success dependency.

quiz.question: Which command creates nested directories in a single operation?
quiz.explanation: -p (parents) creates parent directories as needed. Without -p, mkdir fails if parent doesn't exist.

incorrect_explanations.mkdir dir: Without -p, mkdir only creates a single directory and fails if the parent doesn't exist.
incorrect_explanations.mkdir -r a/b/c: -r is not a valid mkdir flag. The correct flag is -p.

quiz.question: You create app.env and write a database password into it. What should you do immediately after?
options: chmod 777 app.env to make it accessible
chmod 600 app.env to restrict access to owner only
chown root app.env to protect it
quiz.answer: chmod 600 app.env to restrict access to owner only
quiz.explanation: 600 = rw------- means only the owner can read or write. Other users on the system have no access. This protects credentials.

incorrect_explanations.chmod 777 app.env to make it accessible: 777 gives everyone read/write/execute — anyone on the system could read your database password.
incorrect_explanations.chown root app.env to protect it: Transferring ownership to root doesn't help if permissions are too open.
incorrect_explanations.mv app.env /etc/ for system-wide access: Credentials should never be placed in /etc — that directory is often world-readable.

quiz.question: Which operator appends command output to a log file without overwriting existing content?
quiz.explanation: >> appends to the end of the file. > truncates (overwrites) the file completely. This is critical for log files where history must be preserved.

incorrect_explanations.>: > overwrites the entire file — you would lose all previous log entries.
incorrect_explanations.|: | pipes output to another command's input — not to a file.
incorrect_explanations.&: & sends the command to the background — not related to file redirection.

quiz.question: You want to verify that deploy.sh is executable before running it. What do you check?
options: ls -l deploy.sh — look for x in the permission string
quiz.answer: ls -l deploy.sh — look for x in the permission string
quiz.explanation: ls -l shows the permission string. If you see -rwxr-xr-x (x in positions 4, 7, 10), the file is executable.

incorrect_explanations.cat deploy.sh: cat shows file content but not permissions.
incorrect_explanations.file deploy.sh: file shows file type (text, binary) but not permissions.
incorrect_explanations.echo deploy.sh: echo just prints the filename as text — no information about permissions.

quiz.question: What does `ps aux | grep nginx | grep -v grep | wc -l` count?
options: The number of lines in /etc/nginx config
The number of nginx processes currently running
The size of the nginx binary
The number of grep processes running
quiz.answer: The number of nginx processes currently running
quiz.explanation: ps aux lists all processes → grep nginx filters for nginx → grep -v grep removes the grep command itself → wc -l counts remaining lines (= nginx processes).

incorrect_explanations.The number of lines in /etc/nginx config: This pipeline inspects running processes, not config files.
incorrect_explanations.The size of the nginx binary: wc -l counts lines, not file sizes. Use wc -c for bytes.
incorrect_explanations.The number of grep processes running: grep -v grep removes grep itself from the count.

quiz.question: Scenario: Your deploy script ran successfully but the app isn't responding. What is your first check?
options: Check ss -lntp to see if the app is listening on its expected port
quiz.answer: Check ss -lntp to see if the app is listening on its expected port
quiz.explanation: ss -lntp confirms if the application process bound to the expected port. No LISTEN = process crashed or configured wrong port.

incorrect_explanations.Reboot the server: Rebooting destroys the current state and any debugging evidence. Always investigate first.
incorrect_explanations.Delete and redeploy everything: This destroys evidence. Always diagnose before making changes.
incorrect_explanations.Check DNS settings first: DNS issues cause name resolution failures. The first check should be local port binding.

quiz.question: What makes a provisioning script 'idempotent'?
options: It runs faster on each subsequent run
Running it multiple times produces the same result without errors
It only runs once and refuses to run again
It uses variables instead of hardcoded values
quiz.answer: Running it multiple times produces the same result without errors
quiz.explanation: Idempotent scripts are safe to re-run. Commands like mkdir -p and conditional file creation make scripts idempotent — the result is the same whether run once or ten times.

incorrect_explanations.It runs faster on each subsequent run: Speed is not related to idempotency.
incorrect_explanations.It only runs once and refuses to run aga: This is the opposite of idempotent — idempotent means safe to run many times.
incorrect_explanations.It uses variables instead of hardcoded v: Variables improve maintainability but are not related to idempotency.

quiz.question: Which command shows a timestamped audit log of the last 5 deployment events in deploy.log?
quiz.explanation: tail -N shows the last N lines — perfect for viewing recent log entries. Deployments append to the end, so tail shows the most recent ones.

incorrect_explanations.cat deploy.log: cat shows the entire file — impractical for large log files.
incorrect_explanations.head -5 deploy.log: head shows the FIRST 5 lines (oldest entries) — not the most recent.
incorrect_explanations.ls -5 deploy.log: ls is for listing directory contents, not reading file content.

quiz.question: You want to copy app.env with a timestamp in the backup name. Which command is correct?
quiz.explanation: $(date +%Y%m%d) captures the current date in YYYYMMDD format. Combined with cp, this creates a timestamped backup file.

incorrect_explanations.cp app.env app.env.backup: This works but uses a generic name — multiple backups overwrite each other.
incorrect_explanations.mv app.env backups/: mv moves and removes the original — you'd lose the active config file.
incorrect_explanations.copy app.env backups/: copy is a Windows command. Linux uses cp.

quiz.question: What does `2>&1` mean in `./script.sh > output.log 2>&1`?
options: Redirect stderr (2) to the same place as stdout (1) — capturing all output
quiz.answer: Redirect stderr (2) to the same place as stdout (1) — capturing all output
quiz.explanation: File descriptor 1 is stdout, 2 is stderr. 2>&1 merges stderr into stdout so both are written to output.log. Without it, error messages would go to the terminal.

incorrect_explanations.Run the script twice: 2>&1 is a redirection operator, not a loop.
incorrect_explanations.Split output between two files: 2>&1 combines both streams into one destination, not splits them.
incorrect_explanations.Run in background with 2 threads: & at the end runs in background. 2>&1 is only about file descriptors.

quiz.question: Your rollback script needs to find the most recent backup file in backups/. Which command gets the filename?
quiz.explanation: ls lists files. sort alphabetically orders them (timestamps in YYYYMMDD format sort chronologically). tail -1 gets the last line = most recent file.

incorrect_explanations.ls backups/: Lists all files but doesn't select the most recent one.
incorrect_explanations.ls backups/ | sort | head -1: head -1 gets the FIRST (oldest) file after sort, not the most recent.
incorrect_explanations.find backups/ -type f: find lists files but doesn't select the most recent — you'd need additional sorting.

resources.summary: The capstone project brings together all Linux skills: file navigation, permissions, bash scripting, process management, and networking — in a realistic DevOps server provisioning workflow. This is the foundation of Infrastructure as Code and production environment management.

resources.cheat_sheet:
# Linux Capstone Quick Reference

## Project Setup
mkdir -p project/{app/config,scripts,logs,backups}
chmod 600 project/app/config/app.env
chmod 755 project/scripts/*.sh

## Pipe Chaining Operators
cmd1 | cmd2     # Pipe stdout to cmd2
cmd1 && cmd2    # Run cmd2 only if cmd1 succeeds
cmd1 || cmd2    # Run cmd2 only if cmd1 fails
cmd1 ; cmd2     # Always run both
> file          # Redirect (overwrite)
>> file         # Append to file
2>&1            # Merge stderr into stdout

## Logging Pattern
echo "[$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] Event by $(whoami)" >> app.log

## Timestamped Backup
cp config.env backups/config.env.$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M)

## Health Check Pattern
[ -f config.env ] && echo 'Config: OK' || echo 'Config: MISSING'
ss -lntp | grep :PORT && echo 'Port OPEN' || echo 'Port CLOSED'

## Background with Logging
./script.sh > output.log 2>&1 &
tail -f output.log   # Monitor in real-time

## Idempotent Directory Creation
mkdir -p dir    # Safe to run multiple times
[ -d dir ] || mkdir dir   # Only create if missing
[ -f file ] || touch file # Only create file if missing

commands_table.description: Pipe output of cmd1 to input of cmd2.
Run cmd2 only if cmd1 succeeds.
Append command output to a file.
Redirect both stdout and stderr to file.

commands_table.name: tee
commands_table.description: Print output AND save to file simultaneously.

commands_table.name: tail -f
commands_table.description: Follow a log file in real-time.

commands_table.name: wc -l
commands_table.description: Count the number of lines in output.

revision_notes: The project setup order: directories first → files second → permissions third → scripts fourth → verification last.
Always use mkdir -p for nested directories — it's idempotent and never fails on existing directories.
Config files with credentials: 600. Scripts meant to run: 755. Private directories: 700.
>> preserves log history. > destroys it. Never use > for log files.
&& chains steps safely — if one fails, the rest don't run.
best_practices: Build provisioning scripts to be idempotent — safe to run multiple times without side effects.
Always log deployments with timestamps and the triggering user for auditability.
Backup config files before modifying them — use timestamped names for version tracking.
Run a health check script after every deployment before announcing success.
beginner_mistakes: Using ; instead of && to chain dependent steps — if mkdir fails, ; still runs cd.
Forgetting to redirect stderr (2>&1) in background scripts — errors are lost.
Not testing health checks after deployment — services can start partially broken.
Using > instead of >> for log files — destroying deployment history.

interview_questions.question: How would you create an idempotent server provisioning script?
interview_questions.answer: Use mkdir -p for directories (never fails if exists), conditional file creation ([ -f file ] || echo '...' > file), chmod in absolute mode (not relative), and test-first patterns. The script should produce the same result on every run.

interview_questions.question: A deployment succeeded but the app isn't responding. Walk through your debugging steps.
interview_questions.answer: 1) ss -lntp to check the app is listening on the correct port. 2) ps aux | grep appname to verify the process is running. 3) tail -f logs/app.log for error messages. 4) curl -I localhost:PORT to test HTTP response. 5) Check permissions on config files.

books.title: The Phoenix Project — Gene Kim
